name: Spiced Lamb
intro: Kefta means ‘to pummel’ and every country in the Middle East seems to have its unique version. Whether Turkish köfte, Persian koofteh or Arab kefta or kofta, each variation is absolutely delicious and uses ingredients typical of the cuisine of its own culture. This particular version features currants and pine nuts. I love this union because not only do you get a burst of juicy sweetness but the satisfying crunch of toasted pine nuts makes it a winning combination for me. Serve these kefta with rice, naan bread or soft tortilla wraps – and a dollop of thick Greek yogurt really complements them, too.

ingredients.item: large onion, minced or very finely chopped
packets of flat leaf parsley, leaves and stalks finely chopped

method: Preheat a large nonstick frying pan over a medium heat. Preheat the oven to 140°C/fan 120°C/gas mark 1.

Put all the ingredients, except for the oil, into a large mixing bowl. Using your hands mix everything together. Really work the mixture for a good few minutes, taking the time to really pummel the meat until it has broken down and to ensure that the egg and spices are evenly distributed into the mixture.

Take a small handful of mixture, slightly larger than a golf ball, and shape into small, elongated sausage-like koftas, just over 5cm (2in) long, with slightly pointed ends and fatter middles. This is the traditional kefta shape, but you can shape them into small or large patties or simply make meatballs – the choice is yours.

Heat a drizzle of oil in the preheated frying pan over a medium-high heat and fry several of the patties at a time without overcrowding the pan. Don’t flip the kefta over until you see a little brown crust forming on the underside. Brown on all sides, then transfer to an ovenproof dish lined with nonstick baking paper and keep the cooked batches warm in the oven while you fry the remaining kefta.
